title: Rethinking the Value of Initial Claims as a Forecasting Tool

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This New York Fed research by economist Margaret M. McConnell examines the forecasting power of weekly initial unemployment insurance claims for predicting employment changes. The study finds claims are a reliable predictor during recessions but lose predictive power early in economic expansions. The paper attributes this to the breakdown of the inverse relationship between layoffs and hiring during sustained growth periods.
key_points: Initial unemployment claims consistently signal employment changes during recessions but not during expansions
A forecasting model incorporating initial claims improves accuracy during recessions but worsens it during early expansion phases
During recessions, layoffs and hiring are inversely related: falling hiring triggers rising layoffs, and recovery brings falling layoffs
During expansions, this inverse relationship between layoffs and hiring disappears, and hiring becomes the dominant factor in employment changes
The predictive power of initial claims is weak during periods of sustained economic growth
The research was published as part of the Current Issues in Economics and Finance series
why_it_matters: For macro forecasters and policymakers, this research challenges the universal applicability of initial claims as a labor market indicator. During expansions, relying solely on claims data would lead to suboptimal forecasts, as hiring dynamics become more important than layoff signals. This has implications for how traders and analysts interpret weekly jobless claims data in different economic regimes.
macro_context: This research provides a methodological contribution to understanding the conditional reliability of initial jobless claims as an employment forecasting tool. It suggests that the predictive relationship between claims and employment is regime-dependent—strong during downturns but weak during expansions when hiring is the primary driver of employment changes.
